next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The bank sat in the yellow noon like a loaf gone stale on the sill, its brick front filmed with dust where yesterday's wind had struck and dried. Cora tied the mare to the rail, flattened her palm against the animal's damp neck, and waited until the leather smell and the heat coming off the horse quit pushing at her face. If she went in angry, August would let her spend it and call that a victory. She needed to go in hard instead. There was a difference.

She had carried the image for days now. Ezra sat at August's desk with the green blotter under his wrists. August's sleeve had been turned back once because of the heat, and the small white knife on the desk kept opening and shutting in August's fingers while Ezra bent his head and signed. Cora had tried, the first night after seeing it, to tell herself a dust-memory might lie in the way grief lied, by showing the worst shape first. Then Hester's jars had proved the opposite. The dust did not invent. It only refused mercy.

She crossed the sidewalk and went in.

Inside, the bank was cooler by three degrees and meaner for it. It smelled of paper, metal, old sweat, and dust trapped in cloth bindings. The wire of the teller cage cut everybody into squares. A ceiling fan turned with all the conviction of a tired spoon. Behind the wicket, Miss Lenz looked up from a stack of bills, rubbed her thumb and forefinger in the saucer of cornstarch she kept by the cash drawer so the notes would stop clinging in the heat, and gave Cora the cautious smile reserved for women whose names lived in ledgers.

"Mrs. Vale."

"I need...
open_hooks_to_carry: The chapter includes genuine quiet, humor, and non-strategic communal life.
Dell's relation to school is shown as conflicted rather than simplistic contempt.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...arried enough of it alone.

Thea said, very measured now, "If you make him open the file, he'll know you're not merely refusing. He'll know you've scented something."

"He already knows that."

"And if he doesn't show it?"

Cora thought of August's fingers flattening on the form. Of the car by the pump. Of Walter Knapp, decent enough to be unbearable, telling her there might still be shape left for the living. She thought of the word voluntary and felt her stomach turn.

"Then he'll have to tell me why he wants my name so badly," she said. "And if he won't tell it, he can lie to my face while I stand there and listen close."

Thea watched her for a long second. Then, to Cora's surprise, the corner of her mouth lifted. Not because anything was amusing. Because she recognized the choice and could not stop herself from respecting it.

"All right," she said. "Then when you go, go with the exact words. Not with rage. Rage is what he expects from a debtor woman. Questions are harder on men like August."

"I can ask questions."

"You can ask them like nails."

"That too."

Dell, still baffled, said, "Somebody want to tell me what in hell either of you is talking about?"

Neither answered at once. Beans snapped. The kettle settled. Outside, the light went thinner over the yard and the wheat beyond it looked almost silver with dust.

Cora reached under the sugar jar and took August's note out from beneath it. She folded it once and slid it into the pocket of her apron. The paper warmed fast against her belly. Not much. Enough.

"Eat first," she said. "Then we'll see what gets told."
state_deltas_required: Give Morrow's remaining children and adults one human school day before closure decides the town's mood entirely.
Thea chooses to skip her Lincoln interview and stay through the basin fight, committing herself more deeply to Morrow and Cora at real cost to her own escape route.
